children.text: On the Persistence of Fictional Institutions
children.label: section_header

children.text: This chapter argues that fictional institutions persist not because their founding purpose remains intact but because peripheral stakeholders repeatedly repurpose them, a pattern best captured by what I call the Dunmore continuity argument: an institution survives exactly as long as some coalition finds it cheaper to redirect than to dismantle. The archival record examined below traces three such redirections across a single invented institution's fifty-year history.
children.label: text
